topic: discussion about manual therapy in physio curriculum

turns.content: random question but do you know much about physiotherapy education and how curriculums are structured
I have a decent understanding of how physio programmes are structured in the UK. What's on your mind?

so theres this big debate in physio about whether students should learn manual therapy earlier in the course. like proper hands on joint mobilisations soft tissue work all that. at my uni we didnt really get into it until second year and even then it was basic. i feel like we should have started way earlier

That's an interesting debate and it's one that physio educators have been going back and forth on. The argument for starting early is that clinical placements come around fast and students feel underprepared with their hands-on skills. What's the argument you're hearing on the other side?

the other side is like well you need the anatomy and physiology foundation first before you can do manual therapy safely. which i get yeah you need to understand the structures before you start mobilising them. but tbh we were doing anatomy in first year anyway so why not run manual therapy alongside it you know. learn the shoulder anatomy then practice shoulder mobilisations that same week

That actually makes a lot of pedagogical sense -- pairing the theoretical knowledge with practical application in real time. It reinforces both. Learning anatomy in isolation can feel abstract, but if you're simultaneously learning to palpate and mobilise those structures, the anatomy sticks better. Did you find that was the case when you finally started manual therapy?

100% bruv. like i learned more about the glenohumeral joint in one practical session doing mobilisations than i did in weeks of lectures about it. because when youve got your hands on someones shoulder and youre feeling the joint play it just clicks. no pun intended 😂

Ha, well put. There's a lot of evidence supporting that kind of experiential learning -- the motor and tactile experience creates a much deeper understanding than passive learning alone. Do you think there's a safety concern though? First-year students who haven't fully grasped anatomy performing joint mobilisations could be risky.

yeah thats the main pushback and i think its valid to a point. but you could start with low risk stuff init. like grade 1 and 2 mobilisations which are gentle oscillations not end range stuff. and do it supervised in small groups. youre not sending first years out to do spinal manipulations obviously. its about graded exposure same way we teach rehab to patients you know. start simple build up

That's a solid argument -- graded exposure is something physios understand intuitively from clinical practice, so it makes sense to apply the same principle to education. Start with low-grade peripheral joint mobs in a controlled setting, then progress the complexity as the anatomical knowledge deepens. Have you talked to any of your lecturers about this?

yeah actually one of my lecturers agrees with me. she said the problem is timetabling and resources. you need small group sessions for manual therapy and thats expensive because you need more tutors and more clinic space. so unis keep it until later when the cohort has thinned out a bit from people dropping out or switching courses. which is kind of depressing if you think about it

That's a pragmatic reality unfortunately -- curriculum design is often shaped as much by logistics and funding as by what's educationally optimal. The irony is that better early practical exposure might actually reduce dropout rates because students would feel more engaged and connected to the profession from day one.

thats exactly what i said to her!! like bare people on my course thought about quitting in first year because it was all lectures and essays and they were like this isnt what i signed up for. they wanted to be doing physio not just reading about it. if they had manual therapy from week one theyd have been way more motivated

It's a compelling case. You should honestly consider writing about this -- even a reflective piece or a blog post. With your placement experience and your perspective as a student who's lived through the current structure, you've got a legitimate voice on this. It's the kind of topic that programme leads and the Chartered Society of Physiotherapy would engage with.

you know what thats not a bad idea actually. i might bring it up with my personal tutor see if theres a way to feed it into the programme review. they always say they want student feedback but then nothing changes 😂 still worth a shot though. cheers for talking it through with me i feel like my argument is way sharper now

Anytime. Your argument is genuinely strong -- you've got the educational rationale, the safety mitigation with graded exposure, and the retention angle. Put that to your programme review and see what happens. The worst they can say is no, but you might plant a seed that actually changes things for future students.
